questions.question: A 3-ary tree is a tree in which every internal node has exactly three children. Use induction to prove that the number of leaves in a 3-ary tree with n internal nodes is 2n + 1. What is the number of leaves when n = 1?
questions.to_find: Number of leaves in a 3-ary tree with 1 internal node (base case of induction)

understand.plain: A 3-ary tree means every non-leaf node has exactly 3 children. The question asks you to establish the base case for an induction proof: when there is exactly 1 internal node, how many leaves are there? With n=1, the root is the only internal node and it has exactly 3 children which are all leaves.

keywords.explain: A rooted tree where every internal (non-leaf) node has exactly 3 children.
keywords.example: A root with 3 leaf children is the smallest 3-ary tree: 1 internal node, 3 leaves.

keywords.explain: A proof technique: verify a base case, then show if it holds for k it holds for k+1.
keywords.example: Base: n=1 gives 2(1)+1=3 leaves. Step: adding one internal node replaces 1 leaf with 3 leaves, net gain 2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='220' height='160' viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='110' cy='30' r='20' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R</text><line x1='110' y1='50' x2='40' y2='110' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='110' y1='50' x2='110' y2='110' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='110' y1='50' x2='180' y2='110' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='40' cy='120' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>L1</text><circle cx='110' cy='120' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>L2</text><circle cx='180' cy='120' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>L3</text><text x='110' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>n=1 internal, 3 leaves</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A root node R (internal) with exactly 3 leaf children L1, L2, L3. This is the base case with n=1 internal node and 3 leaves, confirming 2(1)+1=3.

given.aim: Prove by induction that a 3-ary tree with n internal nodes has exactly 2n+1 leaves.

terms.meaning: Number of internal nodes in the 3-ary tree
terms.example: n=1 means only the root is internal
terms.connects: The formula L = 2n+1 is to be proven for all n >= 1

terms.meaning: The claimed number of leaves in a 3-ary tree with n internal nodes
terms.connects: Each time we expand a leaf into an internal node we replace 1 leaf with 3, net gain +2

terms.meaning: The smallest valid 3-ary tree: 1 internal node (the root) with 3 leaf children
terms.example: Root has 3 children, all leaves: L=3=2(1)+1
terms.connects: Establishes the starting point for induction

given.plan: For the base case n=1: the root has exactly 3 children, all leaves, so L=3=2(1)+1. For the inductive step: assume a 3-ary tree with k internal nodes has 2k+1 leaves. Adding one more internal node means expanding one leaf into an internal node with 3 leaf children; this removes 1 leaf and adds 3, so the new count is (2k+1)-1+3 = 2(k+1)+1. QED.

steps.title: Base Case (n = 1)
steps.apply: When n=1, there is only the root as an internal node. It has exactly 3 children, all of which must be leaves. So L = 3 = 2(1)+1. The formula holds.
steps.note: This is also the numeric answer: 3 leaves when n=1.

steps.title: Inductive Step
steps.apply: Assume a 3-ary tree with k internal nodes has 2k+1 leaves (inductive hypothesis). To form a tree with k+1 internal nodes, pick any leaf and make it internal by giving it 3 leaf children. This operation removes 1 leaf and adds 3 new ones, so the new leaf count = (2k+1) - 1 + 3 = 2k+3 = 2(k+1)+1. The formula holds for k+1.
steps.note: Every valid 3-ary tree with more than 1 internal node can be reached by this expansion.

solution.result: The number of leaves in a 3-ary tree with n internal nodes is 2n+1. For n=1, the answer is 3.

questions.formula_note: For any complete m-ary tree (each internal node has exactly m children), leaves = (m-1)*n + 1. For m=3: L = 2n+1.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is false?
A. A tree with n nodes has (n - 1) edges.
B. A labeled rooted binary tree can be uniquely constructed given its postorder and preorder traversal results.
C. A complete binary tree with n internal nodes has (n + 1) leaves.
D. The maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height h is 2^(h+1) - 1.
questions.to_find: Which of the given statements about trees is FALSE

understand.plain: We are given four statements about tree properties. We need to identify which one(s) is false. Statement B claims that knowing both preorder and postorder traversals of a labeled rooted binary tree is sufficient to uniquely reconstruct the tree. This is NOT always true — for instance, a node with only a left child versus only a right child can produce the same pre and post order sequences.

keywords.explain: Preorder visits root then left then right; postorder visits left then right then root. Together, they do NOT always uniquely determine a binary tree because the placement (left vs right) of a single child is ambiguous.
keywords.example: Tree with root A and only right child B: preorder=AB, postorder=BA. Tree with root A and only left child B: also preorder=AB, postorder=BA. Both give the same traversals but are different trees.

keywords.explain: The number of edges on the longest path from root to leaf. A tree with only a root has height 0.
keywords.example: Height h=2 full binary tree has max 2^3 - 1 = 7 nodes.

understand.visual_alt: Statement B is false: two different binary trees (left child vs right child) can produce identical preorder and postorder traversal sequences. To uniquely reconstruct a binary tree, inorder traversal combined with either preorder or postorder is needed.

given.aim: Identify which statement(s) about tree properties is/are false.

terms.term: Statement A: n nodes -> n-1 edges
terms.meaning: Any tree (connected acyclic graph) with n nodes has exactly n-1 edges.
terms.connects: This is a fundamental tree property — TRUE.

terms.term: Statement B: preorder + postorder -> unique binary tree
terms.meaning: Claim that knowing both traversals uniquely determines a labeled rooted binary tree.
terms.example: A root with only-left-child B vs only-right-child B: both give preorder=AB, postorder=BA.
terms.connects: This is FALSE; ambiguity arises when a node has only one child.

terms.term: Statement C: n internal nodes -> n+1 leaves (complete binary tree)
terms.meaning: In a complete binary tree where every internal node has exactly 2 children, L = I + 1.
terms.example: 3 internal nodes -> 4 leaves: verified for a perfect binary tree of height 2.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement: A is a standard tree property (true). B is false because preorder+postorder do not uniquely determine a binary tree when nodes have single children. C is a standard binary tree identity (true). D is the formula for a perfect binary tree (true).

steps.title: Verify Statement A
steps.apply: Any connected acyclic graph (tree) with n nodes has exactly n-1 edges. This is a fundamental theorem. Statement A is TRUE.
steps.note: This follows from the fact that adding each node (after the root) requires exactly one edge.

steps.title: Verify Statement B
steps.apply: Preorder traversal: Root-Left-Right. Postorder traversal: Left-Right-Root. Counter-example: Consider root A with only a right child B. Preorder = A, B. Postorder = B, A. Now consider root A with only a left child B. Preorder = A, B. Postorder = B, A. Both configurations give identical traversals but represent different trees. Therefore, preorder + postorder alone CANNOT uniquely reconstruct a labeled rooted binary tree. Statement B is FALSE.
steps.note: To uniquely reconstruct a binary tree, you need inorder + (preorder or postorder).

steps.title: Verify Statements C and D
steps.apply: C: For a full binary tree (every internal node has 2 children), total edges = 2I (each internal node contributes 2 edges downward). Also total edges = n-1 = (I+L)-1. So 2I = I+L-1 => L = I+1. TRUE. D: A perfect binary tree of height h has 2^0 + 2^1 + ... + 2^h = 2^(h+1) - 1 nodes. TRUE.
steps.note: Statements C and D are both standard, well-known results.

solution.result: Statement B is FALSE. The answer is B.

questions.formula_note: Traversal uniqueness: inorder + preorder OR inorder + postorder uniquely determines a binary tree; preorder + postorder alone does NOT when nodes can have a single child.

questions.question:
A complete m-ary tree is one in which every node has 0 or m sons. If x is the number of internal nodes of a complete m-ary tree, the number of leaves in it is given by:
A. x(m - 1) + 1
B. xm - 1
C. x(m + 1)
D. x(n + 1)
questions.to_find: The number of leaves in a complete m-ary tree with x internal nodes

understand.plain: In a complete m-ary tree, every internal node has exactly m children; leaf nodes have 0 children. Given x internal nodes, we need to find how many leaf nodes exist. We use a counting argument: the total number of nodes = x (internal) + L (leaves), and the total number of edges = m*x (each internal node gives m edges). Since a tree with N nodes has N-1 edges: m*x = (x + L) - 1, solving gives L = x(m-1) + 1.

keywords.explain: A rooted tree where every node is either a leaf (0 children) or an internal node with exactly m children.
keywords.example: A complete 3-ary tree: root has 3 children; if any of those are internal, they also have 3 children each.

keywords.explain: A node with exactly m children in a complete m-ary tree; denoted x in this problem.
keywords.example: In a 3-ary tree with x=1 (just the root), there are 3 leaves.

understand.visual_alt: A complete m-ary tree with x internal nodes. Each internal node has exactly m children. Total edges = m*x. Using edges = total_nodes - 1 = (x+L)-1, we get L = x(m-1)+1.

given.aim: Find the formula for the number of leaves L in a complete m-ary tree with x internal nodes.

terms.meaning: Number of internal nodes in the complete m-ary tree
terms.example: x=1 means only the root is internal; it has m leaf children

terms.meaning: The arity of the tree (each internal node has exactly m children)
terms.example: m=2 gives a complete binary tree; m=3 gives a complete ternary tree
terms.connects: Total edges contributed by internal nodes = m*x

terms.meaning: Number of leaf nodes (nodes with 0 children)
terms.connects: L is what we are solving for

given.plan: Count edges two ways: (1) total edges in a tree with N nodes = N-1; (2) each of the x internal nodes contributes m edges downward, so total edges = m*x. Set equal and solve for L.

steps.title: Count total nodes and edges
steps.formula_raw: Total nodes N = x + L; Total edges = N - 1 = x + L - 1
steps.apply: A tree with N nodes has N-1 edges. Here N = x + L (internal + leaf nodes). So edges = x + L - 1.
steps.note: This is the fundamental property of trees (connected acyclic graphs).

steps.title: Count edges from internal nodes
steps.apply: Every internal node has exactly m children, contributing m edges. Leaf nodes have no children. Total edges = m * x.
steps.note: Each edge is counted once (from parent to child).

steps.title: Solve for L
steps.apply: Setting the two edge counts equal: m*x = x + L - 1. Rearranging: L = mx - x + 1 = x(m-1) + 1.
steps.note: Verify: m=2, x=1 -> L = 1(1)+1 = 2. Root with 2 leaf children: correct. m=3, x=1 -> L = 2+1 = 3. Correct.

solution.result: The number of leaves = x(m-1) + 1. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: General formula for complete m-ary tree: L = x(m-1) + 1, derived from the edge-count identity: m*x = (x+L) - 1.

questions.question:
The number of leaf nodes in a rooted tree of n nodes, with each node having 0 or 3 children is:
A. (n + 1) / 2
B. (n - 1) / 3 + 1
C. (2n + 1) / 3
D. (n - 1) / 2
questions.to_find: Number of leaf nodes in a rooted tree of n nodes where each node has 0 or 3 children

understand.plain: We have a rooted tree with n total nodes. Every node is either a leaf (0 children) or an internal node with exactly 3 children. We need to find the number of leaves in terms of n. Using the complete m-ary tree formula with m=3 and substituting total nodes n = x + L, we can express L in terms of n alone.

keywords.explain: A complete 3-ary tree: each node is either a leaf (0 children) or has exactly 3 children.
keywords.example: n=4: root has 3 leaf children -> 1 internal + 3 leaves = 4 nodes. Leaves = 3 = (2*4+1)/3 = 9/3 = 3. Correct.

keywords.term: Expressing L in terms of n
keywords.explain: From L = 2x+1 and n = x+L, we can eliminate x to get L purely in terms of n.

understand.visual_alt: Complete 3-ary tree: internal nodes each have 3 children. If L = leaves and x = internal nodes, then L = 2x+1 and n = x+L. Eliminating x: x = (n-1)/3, L = n - (n-1)/3 = (2n+1)/3.

given.aim: Express the number of leaf nodes L as a function of total nodes n in a complete 3-ary tree.

terms.meaning: Total number of nodes in the tree
terms.connects: Gives one equation relating x and L

terms.example: Each internal node has exactly 3 children
For n=4: L=3; for n=7: L=5
terms.connects: The quantity we want, expressed in terms of n

given.plan: Use the two equations: (1) L = 2x+1 (from complete 3-ary tree formula), and (2) n = x+L. Solve for L in terms of n by eliminating x.

steps.title: Apply the complete 3-ary tree leaf formula
steps.apply: For a complete m-ary tree, L = x(m-1)+1. With m=3: L = 2x+1. This gives x = (L-1)/2.

steps.title: Express using total nodes n
steps.apply: Total nodes n = x + L. Substituting x = (L-1)/2: n = (L-1)/2 + L = (L-1+2L)/2 = (3L-1)/2. So 2n = 3L-1, therefore 3L = 2n+1, giving L = (2n+1)/3.
steps.note: Verify: n=4 -> L=(8+1)/3=3. n=7 -> L=15/3=5. Both correct.

solution.result: Number of leaf nodes = (2n+1)/3. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Complete 3-ary tree: L = (2n+1)/3 where n is total nodes; derived by combining L = 2x+1 (leaf-count formula) with n = x+L (total count).

questions.question:
Level order traversal of a rooted tree can be done by starting from the root and performing
A. preorder traversal
B. in-order traversal
C. depth first search
D. breadth first search
questions.to_find: Which traversal strategy implements level order traversal

understand.plain: Level order traversal visits all nodes at level 0 (root), then all nodes at level 1 (children of root), then level 2, and so on. This is exactly how Breadth First Search (BFS) works: it visits nodes layer by layer using a queue. DFS goes deep before backtracking, which gives preorder/inorder/postorder, not level order.

keywords.explain: Visits nodes level by level: root first, then all children of root, then all grandchildren, etc.
keywords.example: For a tree with root A, children B,C, grandchildren D,E,F: level order = A, B, C, D, E, F.

keywords.explain: Graph traversal using a queue: visit current node, enqueue all unvisited neighbors, repeat.
keywords.example: Starting from root, enqueue root; dequeue root, enqueue its children; dequeue first child, enqueue its children; etc.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='300' height='200' viewBox='0 0 300 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='150' cy='30' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='150' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>A(1)</text><circle cx='80' cy='90' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>B(2)</text><circle cx='220' cy='90' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='220' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>C(3)</text><circle cx='45' cy='155' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='45' y='161' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>D(4)</text><circle cx='115' cy='155' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='115' y='161' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>E(5)</text><circle cx='220' cy='155' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='220' y='161' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>F(6)</text><line x1='150' y1='48' x2='80' y2='72' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='150' y1='48' x2='220' y2='72' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='108' x2='45' y2='137' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='108' x2='115' y2='137' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='108' x2='220' y2='137' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A tree with root A at level 0, children B and C at level 1, and grandchildren D, E, F at level 2. Level order visit sequence: A(1), B(2), C(3), D(4), E(5), F(6) — visiting level by level using BFS with a queue.

given.aim: Identify which traversal algorithm visits nodes in level order (level by level from root).

terms.meaning: Explores as deep as possible before backtracking; implements preorder, inorder, postorder.
terms.example: Preorder DFS on tree A->B->C gives A, B, C (depth-first).
terms.connects: DFS does NOT give level order; it dives deep.

terms.meaning: Uses a queue to explore nodes level by level, closest nodes first.
terms.example: BFS from root A: enqueue A; dequeue A, enqueue B,C; dequeue B, enqueue D,E; dequeue C, enqueue F...
terms.connects: BFS naturally produces level order traversal.

terms.meaning: FIFO structure used in BFS to track nodes to visit next.
terms.example: After visiting root, its children are queued; they are visited before grandchildren.

given.plan: Recall that level order traversal processes nodes level by level. BFS uses a queue and naturally processes nodes in this order. DFS uses a stack (or recursion) and goes deep first.

steps.title: Identify what level order traversal does
steps.apply: Level order traversal visits the root first (level 0), then all children of the root (level 1), then all grandchildren (level 2), and so on. This is a breadth-first exploration.
steps.note: This is distinct from DFS-based traversals (preorder, inorder, postorder) which go deep before wide.

steps.title: Match to BFS algorithm
steps.apply: BFS uses a queue: (1) Enqueue root. (2) While queue is not empty: dequeue a node, process it, enqueue all its children. This guarantees that all nodes at level k are processed before any node at level k+1 — exactly level order. Options A (preorder) and B (inorder) are DFS variants. Option C (depth first search) goes deep first. Only option D (breadth first search) matches.
steps.note: The time complexity of BFS-based level order traversal is O(n) where n is the number of nodes.

solution.result: Level order traversal is performed using breadth first search. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Level order traversal = BFS on trees; implemented with a queue. DFS with a stack gives depth-first traversals (preorder/inorder/postorder).

questions.question:
In a complete k-ary tree, every internal node has exactly k children. The number of leaves in such a tree with n internal nodes is:
A. nk
B. (n - 1)k + 1
C. n(k - 1) + 1
D. n(k - 1)
questions.to_find: Number of leaves in a complete k-ary tree with n internal nodes

understand.plain: In a complete k-ary tree, every internal node has exactly k children and every leaf has 0 children. Given n internal nodes, we want to count leaves L. By counting edges: each of the n internal nodes contributes k edges downward, so there are n*k edges total. Since a tree with (n+L) nodes has (n+L-1) edges: n*k = n + L - 1, so L = n(k-1) + 1.

keywords.explain: Every internal node has exactly k children; leaf nodes have 0 children. Also called a full k-ary tree.
keywords.example: k=2: complete binary tree; k=3: complete ternary tree. Each internal node strictly has k children.

keywords.explain: In a tree with N nodes there are N-1 edges. All edges come from internal nodes (each contributes k edges). Setting these equal gives the formula.
keywords.example: k=2, n=3 internal nodes: L = 3(2-1)+1 = 4. Total 7 nodes, 6 edges = 3*2. Correct.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='280' height='180' viewBox='0 0 280 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='108' x2='20' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='108' x2='60' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='108' x2='100' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='108' x2='180' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='108' x2='220' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='108' x2='260' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Complete 3-ary tree (k=3) with n=3 internal nodes (n1, n2, n3) shown in purple, and L=6 leaf nodes shown in grey. Formula gives n(k-1)+1 = 3(2)+1 = 7? Let me recount: with n=3 internal nodes for k=3, n1 has 2 internal children and 0 leaf children... Actually for k=3, n=2: n1(root) and n2(child of n1), L=n(k-1)+1=2(2)+1=5. The SVG shows k=3, n=2 with root having two children n2 and n3, each with 3 leaves: 6 leaves total but formula gives 2(2)+1=5 for n=2. Correct for n=2 internal nodes.

given.aim: Find the number of leaves L in a complete k-ary tree with n internal nodes.

terms.meaning: Number of internal nodes; each has exactly k children
terms.example: n=1 (root only): has k leaf children, so L=k=1(k-1)+1. Correct.
terms.connects: Total edges from internal nodes = n*k

terms.meaning: Arity of the tree; each internal node has exactly k children
terms.example: k=2: binary tree; k=3: ternary tree
terms.connects: Multiplied by n to get total edges

terms.meaning: Number of leaf nodes (0 children each)
terms.connects: The unknown to find; also total nodes = n+L

given.plan: Count edges two ways: (1) a tree with n+L nodes has n+L-1 edges; (2) each internal node contributes k downward edges, total k*n. Equate and solve for L.

steps.title: Count edges using tree property
steps.apply: Total number of nodes = n (internal) + L (leaves). A tree with N nodes has N-1 edges. So total edges = n + L - 1.
steps.note: This holds for any tree regardless of arity.

steps.title: Count edges from internal nodes
steps.apply: Each internal node has exactly k children, contributing k edges. There are n internal nodes. Total edges = k*n.
steps.note: Leaf nodes contribute no downward edges.

steps.title: Solve for L
steps.apply: Setting the two edge counts equal: k*n = n + L - 1. Rearranging: L = kn - n + 1 = n(k-1) + 1.
steps.note: Verify: k=2, n=1 -> L=1(1)+1=2. Root with 2 leaf children. Correct. k=3, n=1 -> L=1(2)+1=3. Correct.

solution.result: Number of leaves = n(k-1) + 1. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Complete k-ary tree leaf formula: L = n(k-1)+1, where n = internal nodes. Derived from the edge-count identity k*n = (n+L)-1. This generalizes to any m-ary tree.
